statusLine: EU fuel reserves are under strain in March 2026, with jet fuel the weakest link and a majority of member states holding at least one fuel type below the 90-day mandatory minimum.
fullAnalysis:
As of March 2026, EU-wide average oil stock levels sit at 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87.0 days for jet fuel. While the petrol average nominally clears the 90-day mandatory minimum, both diesel and jet fuel fall short at the aggregate level — a concerning position given that emergency reserve drawdowns linked to ongoing Middle East supply disruptions have already been recorded across several member states. The overall system status is classified as watch, but the headline averages obscure significant stress at the country level.

Jet fuel is the most acutely exposed fuel type. Of the 27 countries in the dataset, 20 of 27 hold jet fuel stocks at a critical level — below the mandatory minimum threshold — including major aviation hubs such as Portugal (21.2 days), Poland (22.5 days), Austria (31.9 days), Finland (31.9 days), and Hungary (31.2 days). Spain, France, Germany, Italy, and the Netherlands all show jet fuel reserves well below 90 days. Only Ireland, Estonia, Slovenia, Croatia, and Sweden report jet fuel stocks clearly above the minimum. This pattern suggests that aviation fuel supply chains are bearing a disproportionate share of the supply-side pressure stemming from the late-February escalation in the Middle East.

Diesel reserves present a similarly uneven picture. Standout performers such as Finland (197.7 days), the Netherlands (207.5 days), and Estonia (124.3 days) lift the EU average, but 15 of 27 countries hold diesel stocks in critical territory, including Romania (30.2 days), Slovakia (33.5 days), and Luxembourg (58.5 days). Petrol reserves are somewhat more resilient in aggregate, though 13 of 27 countries remain critically low, with Luxembourg (38.0 days), Romania (40.4 days), and Lithuania (47.0 days) the most exposed. The Baltic and Nordic states, by contrast, show comparatively robust petrol holdings.

On the price side, EU average petrol stands at €1.771/L and diesel at €1.781/L as of mid-June 2026, with Brent crude at $79.15/barrel — up 9.2% since the pre-escalation weekly close of $72.48 on 23 February 2026. The crude price rise has not yet translated into uniform retail price spikes: Malta (€1.34/L) and Poland (€1.408/L) remain well below the EU average, likely reflecting national subsidy or tax mechanisms, while Denmark (€2.392/L) and the Netherlands (€2.236/L) are the most expensive markets. Any further upward movement in Brent, combined with continued reserve drawdowns, would tighten headroom further — particularly for the cluster of central and southern European countries already operating well below mandatory minimums.
keyPoints: Both diesel (88.1 days) and jet fuel (87.0 days) fall below the EU's 90-day mandatory minimum at the aggregate level, with only petrol (95.6 days) nominally compliant.

Jet fuel is the most stressed commodity: 20 of 27 EU member states hold jet fuel stocks at a critical level, including major aviation markets such as Portugal (21.2 days), Poland (22.5 days), and Spain (35.6 days).

Diesel reserves are critically low in 15 of 27 countries, with Romania (30.2 days), Slovakia (33.5 days), and Luxembourg (58.5 days) among the most exposed despite high EU-average figures bolstered by Finland and the Netherlands.

Brent crude has risen 9.2% since late February 2026 ($72.48 → $79.15/barrel), adding cost pressure on top of already-depleted strategic reserves that have been partially released in response to Middle East supply disruptions.

A small group of countries — Estonia, Ireland, Latvia, and Denmark — maintain broadly comfortable multi-fuel reserve positions, while Luxembourg, Romania, Poland, Austria, and Portugal face critical shortfalls across all three fuel types simultaneously.
